speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We-- we got you married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a baby. And then we-- we started, my uncle was starting to-- to-- we wanted to go to the-- to the United States. So my uncle-- my uncle started to work on papers. He wanted to hire us. He made up a-- an affidavit, I think it was called. And he send the affidavit to us, and we were going to come. We were going to-- it then took a couple years or some more. But we were-- we-- we came to his house, to United-- to Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you something, please? When you were living with this community of survivors--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hanover?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, what was their feeling about religion and about God? Did anybody stay-- stay observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, that came holidays. When we got together in one of the rooms, from where we stayed. And whoever wanted to come, came. There was no such a thing. There was holiday, whenever we knew the holidays are here, and we told-- we went to daven. We had also they got married. People got married. Then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get married with a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a rabbi, sure. Sure, with a rabbi. You can get actually, a lot of people got married. And he took 10 people and one of you get chuppah kiddush, and you're finished, you know, you're married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. A chuppah kiddush means just to say the prayers and go through the ceremony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find-- did-- were there people that kept Shabbos and kosher food?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was kosher. There was butchers. There was kosher food, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was all available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. People, survivors have opened business, yeah, some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there many that expressed anger at God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that they didn't want anything to do with religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they-- didn't-- have-- want to have anything to do with religion. This I don't know. I can tell you only about me and my husband. We still believe in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we thought, we still believe in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now let's get you over to America with your little family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so we-- we arrived to Brooklyn, and we stayed at my uncle's house for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name, your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol Weitzman. He's not with us anymore. He died like three years ago. He was 96.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live, what part of Brooklyn? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in-- it was a funny name. In-- no-- if I know. East Parkway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Eastern Parkway, Crown Heights?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh, gee, in Brooklyn it was-- on Utica Avenue and East Flatbush. East Flatbush.

speaker: interviewer
text: East Flatbush.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he had fish stores when we came to the United States. He had fish stores, a few. And he also had real estate. In-- in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He had three children, grown. My cousins was at that time in college. My older-- he had two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry, we were just went out with them. We are close with him. And there was a daughter. He had a daughter who died a couple years ago, Sylvia. And then is Cy, who lives in Brooklyn Heights. And then we had-- I had-- then I had my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her-- her name was Kaila.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaila what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married-- Kaila, first name. Her married name was Geller. We stayed with her for a few-- a little while when we came to United States. It was the-- the vacation time. And my uncle had his sons home. And at that time he said, you know, you go, you stay with Tanta Kaila, and you come to eat at my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: She also lived in that same neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She lived in New Lots. So she said, OK. So I took with-- after-- after that, they were arrived, after everybody was already got to know each other, and we ate and we drink and everything, we celebrated. She said, you coming with-- you're going to come to my house to stay with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my Aunt Kaila, Aunt Kaila. Then-- and I said, OK, whatever, you know, whatever-- whatever you do, whatever you want. So OK, so we went to her house. And we stayed there for a-- for a week, maybe. Every day my-- my cousin Harry came to pick us up. And we ate in my uncle's house. And one day, I guess she had enough of us. [LAUGHS] She should rest in peace. I didn't mean it. So she said, look. She had a small house, really too. And her son was still-- lived with her too. And it wasn't the easiest. She was an elderly woman. And it wasn't easy for her. I cannot-- you know, I must admit that. And so she said, you know, your uncle sent papers for you. You have to go and stay with your uncle, with uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my cousin came to pick us up, I told him, and I cried a little. So he said, why are you crying? And I couldn't speak a word of English. But I said, well, you know I think-- I-- really nothing. I didn't really want to tell him the story. But-- so he made a stop at one of the fish stores, my uncle worked. And here's my-- is my uncle's wife too. She always was doing the business, especially for the-- this was like summertime, you know. Were they religious people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like us, like us. And so I guess he told his father I-- I don't know-- I'm crying, why I'm crying. So I-- so he asked me in Yiddish. He said, why you-- what's the-- what's the matter? I said, I don't know. Tanta Kaila says she-- I should stay with you in your house. Really, he said. Yes. So right away he said to his son-- yeah, we had a crib for the baby, and all. He said, go and bring everything what for Enid's stuff, and bring to the house, and she stay-- going to stay with us. They had the finished basement in that house, at that time, they didn't feel like-- giving-- like it was a-- a room with a kitchen. And so we stayed there for-- 6-7 months or so, until we find an apartment

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 1949?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we came to the United States in '49, yeah. If it was 1949 maybe already it was beginning of '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you remember was it cold? What was the season when you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we moved from uncle? Oh, I think it was wintertime already, like cold. Yeah, it was like in January or so. And then we-- my-- my husband's sister came from Germany to here an aunt in the-- in Bronx. So she came. She came there. And when he and she got an apartment in the Bronx. So we moved also. We find an apartment in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the Bronx?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the Bronx. And then my husband, from the Bronx, we stayed about five years or so maybe less. I had another child, my youngest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the oldest one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot. I still have my mind. [LAUGHS] And then-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Leon doing all this time to earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for in a factory. He traveled from Bronx to New Lots. He-- first he worked at-- he find a place, I think, he had second cousin or so. They made-- they were working making hats, ladies hats. And then for some reason he didn't want him. So he sent him home. And then-- so my uncle, one of my cousins, my-- my Aunt Kaila's son was a-- was a-- worked for the steel company, the-- a steel company, a very famous steel company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bethlehem Steel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bethlehem Steel, I think. And so he took him, and he was like a foreman there, my cousin. So he took--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a what, a foreman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he took--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? May as well give him credit. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is-- Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just saw him at the part-- Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Geller. Sam Geller, so he took Leon in, and he was traveling from Brooklyn from--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Bronx.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bronx to New Lots every day, back and forth. And on Saturday, he'd make money, $35 a week. And I had two children. And then somebody, there was an ad in the paper that somebody is looking for knitters.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Knitting. Knitting. And my husband used to-- his profession was knitting in Lodz socks. So he answered the ad, and another person answered the ad. And they went there, and they worked there. And those two people, Henry--

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Newman?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Newman worked there, and my husband. And after a couple years working in there-- and he was-- Newman was the knitter, and my husband was the finisher. And there were others, but that's just what they did. And they-- they decided maybe they should go into business themselves. And that's exactly what they did. And then they opened the business. The first shop was in Hackensack, small shop on State Street. And then we lived still in the-- we lived in the Bronx. And we moved to Teaneck. And we took an apartment. And then we bought a house in Teaneck, in then we stayed until-- in Teaneck, until we built this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You built this house how long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They built--

speaker: interviewer
text: 15 years ago, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was-- 19-- 1981, the end of '81. And then they moved the factory to-- to Patterson. And then they moved from Patterson, the business to Little Falls. And then that's when they bought a-- they built their own building. And they had the building. And that's when the partnership ended, in Little Falls. But we're still partners, because of the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you've retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now retired, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- are you able to tell if there's anything, the way you run your life or things that you feel about that's affected by the war? You know, do you see that you do things differently or feel differently than Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what affected me is education. I had-- I didn't have a real education. We came to the United States. And I was-- had one little guy. We had to move out when I was pregnant. And we had very little money. We had an apartment. So I couldn't go to night school. So my husband went. He was the bread giver. He had to know how to spell and how to read. And I-- mine was supposed to be later on, and later on [LAUGHS] never came for me. I was-- I don't know. So this has affected me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it affect your attitude then to giving your children an education?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did. Because I want them to be educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Both are-- both are doctors. My older one is a doctor, an internist. My younger one is a surgeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older one lives in the city, and my younger one lives in Wayne, and is affiliated with Wayne General. And you're going to meet them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have children-- grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my eldest son has two sons. Michael is the oldest. And Max is the younger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife's name is Susan, not Susie. You're Susie, she's Susan, a lovely person. And my younger one has had two marriages. The first marriage was he had a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeremy. And then he-- they were divorced. And then he married his wife now. With now, and they have two beautiful children, a boy and we have a princess, a little girl in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's-- what's the next prince's the prince name, the next boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max.

speaker: interviewer
text: Max. And what the wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amy and Melinda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amy is the baby. Melinda is the wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, Amy is the baby. Lovely, lovely. We've had better luck with daughter-in-laws with my grandchildren. We're really lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you would like to say or something that you would like to teach your children and grandchildren that you may have learned as a result of the Holocaust experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can-- the only message I can give my children is there should be people like us. You know, believe in God, believe in-- in honesty. Believe not to tell lies. To tell the truth is the best way. And I-- we encourage them to become-- to further their education. Even my grandson, I just not-- I was just talking. We had him for dinner here. I said, Michael, he wants to be-- he wants to be a journalist. And I said, Michael, journalist, you've got to go to be a very good one. Otherwise, you'll starve. Right now, you're a young guy. You don't care for money. But when you get older and you get married, you'll need a family. You need to support a family. That's going to be a problem if you're not going to reach the top. He said, I know, Grandma, I know. But I still want to be this. I want to speak, and he gave a beautiful speech at our party, when we were celebrating the 50th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. He made a beautiful-- not a written speech. Just-- he didn't feel-- he didn't think of making a speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still in touch with people that you went through this experience with, any survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm in touch with Mikhal in-- in Israel. I speak, we write to each other. We've been so many times in Israel, always there we see. I was even in touch with my-- older sister was engaged to be married. And the war broke out. And he was-- and the-- and the-- the boyfriend went to the army. And my sister stayed behind. So we did-- we were crying when he was going-- they took him to the army. Because you never know if you come back from the army. Meanwhile, he came back from the army, and my-- my sister's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is-- his name is Moishe, Moishe Dub.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Jerusalem-- in not Jerusalem, in Tel Aviv. And-- in Tel Aviv-- no what is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel, he lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, yes. But then I know the name of the town, Holon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about giving your children and grandchildren a Jewish Education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, I feel that they-- I gave my children, if I would have to do the same thing over again, and have what I have today, I would send them to yeshiva. But at that time, my children went to-- needed a Hebrew school, I send them to the temple for four years. They went four years. Each one of them went for four years of Hebrew school. And they learn how to [NON-ENGLISH] and this, and then-- then till after the bar mitzvah. I had nothing-- nothing to say about my grandchildren. Because they have parents and they have to-- whatever they say is their-- is their business now. My oldest son is more religious than my youngest son. He likes more-- is more like us and so is his children, especially the youngest, my younger grandson. He loves to be-- he has finished Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's now 16, going on 17. He has been-- he also went to Hebrew high in school, in temple. And he loves it. He's very much involved. My older grandson, since he went-- started-- he went off to college, he's already-- he started to have ideas of his own about religion and this and that. But every time he's-- if he thinks something bad, really he thinks of us. And he says, my parents, my grandparents went through so much because they're Jewish, I have to remain Jewish. We'll see. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken much about your story to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: The honest truth, my own children didn't want to-- didn't want to hear that. Where every time we started to talk about it, they didn't ask us questions. And they really didn't want to-- they didn't want us to tell them. I assume the reason was is like that it probably would hurt them to hear that. So they'd rather not-- I mean they know-- knew everything that was going on. I mean little by little, we-- every time we had-- had company, we were all survivors. And we were eating, and-- and smiling, and laughing, I mean smiling and laughing, and talking, and the end of the conversation we ended with the Holocaust. That was even to this day. No matter how good of a time we have, we go to parties, and we're dancing like normal. When we start at the end, we all talk about-- my mother didn't live to see it, my father didn't live to see it. This didn't-- my sister didn't live to see it. This is-- they're always in our mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the earlier years, or, I don't know even now, did you cry a lot or did you cry yourself to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I-- we were like different people. We were just-- we knew this happened, and we couldn't-- yes, we dreamed a lot. I cry more now than then. This is older I get, when I talk about it, I cry-- I cry more now. At that time, I guess, young age, it's different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're too busy just making a new life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And thank God, we made-- I made myself a very good, beautiful life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me before that you missed the closure, not knowing exactly what happened with your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to know, very much so. But the-- the people I-- I met from my town, nobody knows anything from my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we hope that this story that you told us-- and we really appreciate your giving of yourself so much-- we hope that this will be a memorial to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so. And I have to say again that this Polish lady [BACKROUND NOISE] is smarter than anybody I can think of, what she did for me. Because I don't think-- without her-- without her family that they agree to it. Because she had to have-- if they would find me, she would lose the family, and she-- they would be all killed. So I can't tell you how thankful, how thankful I have-- I am to this family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever visit her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, since I left, I didn't visit her. I made a big mistake. We once went to Poland, and it wasn't close where she lives. But I really wanted to go to-- to visit her. But some-- for some reason when we came to Poland, I was afraid to open my mouth that I really-- that I am-- that I was born In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We went back about 10 years ago. So and when I came home, I told her, I was in Poland, in Warsaw, and in Lodz, and in Krakow. And she was so angry at me, and she was so right. I met her grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was working for the Polish-- Polish-- on the boat. He was traveling for the Polish army or whatever, or this was his job really. And he came-- they were transporting something or so. And he came to the United States. He made stops in the United States from Poland and the United States. So one-- the first time he came, we was-- he called me. Like, she has-- she has my telephone number. He came to town, and he called me, and he tell me who he is. And I called-- and he say, he wants to see me. And I said, come to my house. We'll pick you up from the boat. He was stationed-- they stopped in-- in here in-- where is it? Newark is the-- no not Newark. It's where the boats-- boats come in from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was in Newark. That was-- he would probably-- where is this, and which-- which-- where do they stop the boats here?

speaker: interviewer
text: In New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Jersey, yeah. Hoboken. Hoboken, I think, or something. So first time, so he didn't want to come. He said, you come, you know. But then he only came for overnight, you know. So it was that they had to go back. And then again, and then he called every time he came in. And then finally, one day, he stayed overnight. So we went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We went to-- in Greenpoint is all the-- I think it's Greenpoint-- all the Polish people live, a lot of Polish people? Like who came, the-- the newcomers. So-- so he gave me an address of a restaurant. And he said, this is where I'm going to be. Wait for me there. And I never met him. But never in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you hadn't met him before, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So I didn't really know how he looks or what. So but-- he-- he walked in, and we were sitting, as two people like, you know? And he said, I assume you are the-- you are-- you are the Hensons. I said, yeah. He spoke a little English. And we had lunch together. And we-- I said, would you like me to-- you want to come to my house with me?

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was the son that was with you the whole time during the war too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a grandson of my Polish lady. Yeah. So now he doesn't come here anymore. He has-- he got married, and he lives in Poland for-- for good. Or he wants to go to Australia, I hear, sometimes. She writes to me once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're in touch with her all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But for the last couple months, like six, seven months, I don't hear from her. So I did-- I made her as a righteous. She became a righteous for all she did to me. And I wanted to go with-- take her to Israel. Because I think in Israel, that's what I explained to her at the time. She became-- she got a medal in Poland, you know, for the consulate Polish-- the Israel consulate. But I don't think they're supposed to have their name at the Yad Vashem in Israel, in Jerusalem. But I think they said to her something like this. That if you're there, when you're there, they put your name. You have to be-- even not her-- even her sons or so. They can-- they'll put their name in the-- you know, on-- on the wall, and they have this. But I hope I'm going to do it. If it's not-- but then the war broke, then we all think-- that was just last year, at the beginning of the summer of this year, or at the end of the summer, all the bombing starting to-- with the buses and this. I didn't want to take her. She didn't want to go, and I wouldn't want to-- didn't want to go myself. But see, there's one thing what I'm questioning sometimes myself. It's nice I never heard from her sons. Through the letters, I always send them, every letter I send regards, for Henek, for Roma, for the families. Already they were married. They have grandchildren. She's even had great-grandchildren. And I think-- and I always said, I wonder why we still write anyway. She and I, she used to write me back, yeah, they saw me today. They came-- they don't live in the same place anymore. And they saw me too, like this day Roma came or or Henek came, or-- and the grandchildren. But they're all separate. They didn't live in the same area. For a while, they did live together in the same town with the younger son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he died, and then things became a little tougher for her. And I still question myself why. Maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's say, when I-- when she became a righteous, I wrote to her-- I said, are your children happy that you became a righteous? She never gave me an answer. So I question. Maybe they were not so happy what she did. Never know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to show pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I have the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we have to stop, because we have to set up the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, I want--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just say with the mother, no? Yeah. First, this is my mother and father and my two sisters. The first name, my mother's name is-- is Bayla. Last name is Weitzman. My father's name-- I don't see it. My father's name is Eli. And his first-- and the second name is Jukin. People of photograph-- that's the people of the photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's the little one in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little one-- my two sisters' names, the one is Tone, the older one. And the younger one is Chaya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you figure out what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean they're little. Look at the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1920?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I don't know, I don't want to say it. Oh, but I gave my mom-- my mother's maiden name, Weitzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's good?

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a good-looking woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a good-looking woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, nice pretty.

speaker: interviewer
text: This class or whatever, and then you hone in on your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, like I say, well, all I can-- I don't remember all the people's names. But this is-- my-- my oldest sister's friends. My sister's name is Tone Jukin. So I don't know the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in Poland, Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second, the second from-- the-- what is the right?

speaker: interviewer
text: The right, and in the bottom row?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom row. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old was she here?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was here, maybe 18 or 19 years old. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are friends of hers or school friends or regular friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not school friends, she was a [NON-ENGLISH].
